content.full_text:
March 14, 1980


Ms. Celia Sweeden
Office of the City Clerk
P.O. Bin 1701
El Centro, California 92244

Dear Ms. Sweeden:

     In your card of February 1, 1980, you ask whether you may
destroy campaign statements filed by candidates for municipal
office in years prior to the effective date of the Political
Reform Act ("the Act"), January 7, 1975.

     The preservation of statements filed since the effective
date of the Act is governed by Government Code §§1009. That
section, however, imposes its requirements with respect to
"campaign statements," a term defined in the Act to mean "an
itemized report which is prepared on a form prescribed by the
[Fair Political Practices] Commission and which provides the
information required by Chapter 4 of this title" (see Govt.
Code §82006). "This title" is Title 9 of the Government Code,
effective January 7, 1975 (see Govt. Code §91016).

     Campaign statements filed prior to January 7, 1975, or under
the provisions of law in effect prior to that time are not
"campaign statements" as defined in Government Code §82006. They
could not have been prepared on forms prescribed by the Fair
Political Practices Commission, nor do they provide the informa-
tion required by Chapter 4 of Title 9, a title effective after
the date of the filing of the statements in question. The pre-
servation and disposition of such statements is not governed,
therefore, by Government Code §81009.

     Prior law governing the disclosure of campaign finances is
contained in Elections Code §§11500-11631. The legislature has
declared that these sections are inoperative unless the campaign
reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act are invalidated
or otherwise rendered inoperable (see Elections Code §11620).
However, the referenced Elections Code sections are to be consid-
ered in full force and effect with respect to any activities

Ms. Celia Sweaden                    -2-                    March 14, 1980


covered by them which occurred prior to January 7, 1975, and to
campaign statements required to be filed by Section 11621 of the
Elections Code (see Stats. 1975, c. 145, §1, effective June 28,
1975, codified as Elections Code §11620).

     The retention and disposition of the statements about which
you inquire, then, appears to be governed by the provision of
the Elections Code under which such statements were filed. Out
response to your question must be limited to the application of
the Political Reform Act to the issues you raised. For advice as
to the requirements of the Elections Code or other law I would
suggest you consult your city's corporation counsel, the county
counsel, or the Attorney General.

     I hope this response has been helpful. If you have additional
questions about requirements of the Political Reform Act, you may
call me at 916/322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        John Giansello
                                        Staff Services Analyst

JG:bw

sections.question_synthetic: May the City Clerk destroy campaign statements filed by municipal candidates prior to the January 7, 1975 effective date of the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign statements filed prior to January 7, 1975 are not governed by the Political Reform Act's preservation requirements, as they do not meet the Act's definition of "campaign statements." The retention and disposition of such pre-1975 statements are governed instead by the Elections Code provisions under which they were originally filed, requiring consultation with corporation counsel or the Attorney General for specific guidance.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with some legibility issues. Date header shows 'March 1A, 1950' which appears to be OCR error for 'March 14, 1980' based on referenced letter date of February 1, 1980. Question and conclusion sections clearly identifiable. Sender identified as John Giansello, Staff Services Analyst.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that campaign statements filed prior to January 7, 1975 (the effective date of the Political Reform Act) are not subject to the Act's preservation requirements since they do not meet the Act's definition of "campaign statements." The disposition of such statements is instead governed by Elections Code provisions in effect at the time they were filed.
